title: Supraworld
anchor_summary: A first-person puzzle-metroidvania built around dense interconnected exploration, ability-gated progression, environmental puzzle solving, secret hunting, and relatively light combat.

similar_games.title: Supraland
similar_games.why_similar: The clearest comp: first-person puzzle-metroidvania exploration, ability unlock gating, toy-world traversal, secret-heavy maps, and light combat.

similar_games.title: Supraland Six Inches Under
similar_games.why_similar: Same series formula with interconnected first-person exploration, upgrade-driven progression, environmental puzzles, and constant off-path discoveries.

similar_games.title: Metroid Prime Remastered
similar_games.why_similar: Shares the first-person metroidvania backbone: interconnected world design, backtracking with new abilities, scanning/exploration, and combat supporting progression.

similar_games.title: Journey to the Savage Planet
similar_games.why_similar: First-person exploratory adventure focused on unlocking traversal tools, revisiting areas for upgrades, platforming, secrets, and light combat in an open-feeling world.

similar_games.title: The Talos Principle 2
similar_games.why_similar: A close puzzle-design neighbor: first-person environmental puzzle solving in wide, discovery-driven spaces with minimal hand-holding, though less metroidvania-structured.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored official descriptions are specific and consistent: the game explicitly calls itself a 'First-Person-Puzzle-Metroidvania' with an interconnected world that opens up through newly found upgrades and abilities, plus heavy emphasis on exploration, secret areas, environmental puzzle solving, and progression-gated traversal. The text repeatedly frames combat as a relatively small, sometimes puzzle-heavy component, which supports metroidvania over an action-led archetype. Detective cases and mystery-solving are notable topical layers, but the core gameplay identity is still ability-gated exploration in a handcrafted interconnected world.

title: Marathon
anchor_summary: A PvPvE extraction FPS built around scavenging runs, risking gear, exfilling with loot, and using that haul to strengthen future loadouts and builds across session-based matches.

similar_games.title: Escape from Tarkov
similar_games.why_similar: The clearest extraction-shooter comp: high-stakes raids, loot-and-exfil structure, persistent gear risk, and progression tied to what you bring out.

similar_games.title: Hunt: Showdown 1896
similar_games.why_similar: Another PvPvE extraction shooter where tense traversal, AI threats, rival squads, and exfil pressure drive each match.

similar_games.title: Marauders
similar_games.why_similar: A session-based sci-fi extraction FPS with squad play, looting, hostile NPCs, enemy crews, and gear-on-the-line runs.

similar_games.title: Call of Duty: Warzone 2.0
similar_games.why_similar: Its DMZ mode shares the accessible extraction loop of infil, loot, PvPvE encounters, missions, and extracting valuable gear.

similar_games.title: Arena Breakout: Infinite
similar_games.why_similar: Another modern extraction FPS centered on tactical raids, scavenging, inventory risk, and run-to-run progression through successful exfils.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently and explicitly identify Marathon as a 'PvPvE survival extraction FPS' and a 'PvP extraction shooter.' The core loop is clearly stated: infiltrate/scavenge zones, fight rival players and hostile AI, loot valuables, exfil, then use stolen loot and season-long unlocks to improve future runs. Supporting details include solo or crew-based runs, in-progress lobby insertion, finite supplies, ranked play, moddable weapons, build crafting, vault space expansion, and persistent seasonal progression. This strongly matches extraction_shooter over generic FPS labels or arena/hero shooter near-misses.

resolved_similar_games.why_similar: The clearest extraction-shooter comp: high-stakes raids, loot-and-exfil structure, persistent gear risk, and progression tied to what you bring out.

Another modern extraction FPS centered on tactical raids, scavenging, inventory risk, and run-to-run progression through successful exfils.

similar_games.title: Vigor
similar_games.why_similar: Third-person extraction loop with scavenging runs, hostile players, bringing loot home, and shelter-based crafting/progression.

similar_games.title: Hunt: Showdown 1896
similar_games.why_similar: PvPvE extraction structure centered on tense raids, AI threats, stealth/noise management, and escaping with valuable rewards.

similar_games.title: Escape from Tarkov
similar_games.why_similar: Defining extraction-shooter template: raid for loot, survive PvPvE encounters, then use stash, traders, quests, and gear progression between runs.

similar_games.why_similar: Sci-fi PvPvE extraction shooter with contracts, persistent loot progression, hostile creatures, and dangerous encounters with other players.

similar_games.why_similar: Third-person cover shooting, gear optimization, and hub-based progression make it a sensible neighbor, though it is broader looter-shooter than pure extraction.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently and concretely describe ARC Raiders as a third-person multiplayer extraction game: players enter distinct topside maps, scavenge loot, survive hostile machines and rival Raiders, then return to the underground hub/workshop to sell loot, craft, repair, upgrade stations, learn blueprints, level skills, and take trader quests. The text explicitly supports solo or up-to-3-player play, PvPvE pressure, repeated incursions with changing map conditions, and progression through gear, crafting, quests, and a branching skill tree. That combination strongly supports extraction_shooter as the core gameplay identity, with loot_shooter and tactical_shooter as secondary affinities rather than primary genre fits.

resolved_similar_games.why_similar: Third-person extraction loop with scavenging runs, hostile players, bringing loot home, and shelter-based crafting/progression.

title: Solid Void - Topsy Turvy World
anchor_summary: A cozy single-player puzzle game built around a two-step loop: solve nonogram/picross-style logic grids, then assemble image sections as jigsaw puzzles to gradually restore a larger artwork.

similar_games.title: Picross S+
similar_games.why_similar: Very close on the core nonogram logic-solving loop, with lots of standalone grids and steady difficulty ramping.

similar_games.title: Glass Masquerade
similar_games.why_similar: Matches the calm, artwork-focused jigsaw side, with relaxing presentation and puzzle-by-puzzle image restoration.

similar_games.title: Murder by Numbers
similar_games.why_similar: Also centers on picross-style deduction, packaged as a more structured progression through many puzzles.

similar_games.title: Glass Masquerade 2: Illusions
similar_games.why_similar: Another art-driven jigsaw puzzler emphasizing visual assembly, atmosphere, and bite-sized puzzle completion.

similar_games.title: Piczle Cross: Story of Seasons
similar_games.why_similar: A cozy, approachable nonogram game with recognizable image reveals and a relaxed cadence similar to this title’s mental-training appeal.

However, the available Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype list does not include a general nonogram/jigsaw puzzle category, and the game does not materially fit hidden_object_puzzle, word_puzzle_strategy, or any other listed archetype. Rejected due to taxonomy coverage mismatch rather than lack of gameplay evidence.

title: Minos
anchor_summary: A reverse-dungeon roguelite where you build and reconfigure trap-filled labyrinths to kill incoming heroes, blending maze design, trap chaining, and defense against repeated runs.

similar_games.title: Meet Your Maker
similar_games.why_similar: Centered on building lethal maze-like outposts packed with traps and routing intruders through killboxes.

similar_games.title: Legend of Keepers
similar_games.why_similar: A roguelite twist on reverse dungeon defense where you prepare traps and monsters to stop invading heroes.

similar_games.title: War for the Overworld
similar_games.why_similar: Focuses on constructing and defending an evil dungeon, placing rooms, traps, and defenses against intruders.

similar_games.title: Dungeon Keeper 2
similar_games.why_similar: Classic reverse-dungeon management with dungeon construction, trap placement, and hero repulsion as the core loop.

similar_games.title: Orcs Must Die! 3
similar_games.why_similar: Emphasizes chaining traps and environmental hazards to wipe out incoming waves, though with less maze-building emphasis.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently define Minos as a maze-building roguelite centered on designing and redesigning labyrinth layouts, placing walls/gates/corridors, linking traps through pressure-plate chain reactions, and defending against invading adventurers across repeatable runs. The clearest gameplay identity is systemic construction and trap engineering rather than direct action combat, which aligns best with engineering_sandbox_sim. Roguelite run structure, procedural variation ('Every run brings new enemies, new traps'), and single-player premium replayability are all directly supported. Some presentation details like camera/perspective are not stated, so those fields are left blank.

resolved_similar_games.why_similar: Centered on building lethal maze-like outposts packed with traps and routing intruders through killboxes.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Store and aggregator copy consistently describe a third-person action-RPG structure: exploring the area around Zoz, helping townspeople through quests, real-time slash-and-dodge combat with a machete, companions with unique combat skills, cooking for buffs, bond-building with residents, and magical weapon/tool enhancement. The strongest fit is a fantasy action RPG with quest exploration and social/progression layers. 'Open world' is not stated outright, so confidence is moderate rather than maximal, but the overall gameplay identity aligns best with open_world_fantasy_action_rpg over alternatives like loot_action_rpg or monster_hunter_style_action_rpg, which lack strong loot-chase or boss-hunt evidence here.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored official descriptions consistently define The Occultist as a first-person narrative thriller with explicit survival-horror framing: 'Survival horror: The Occultist has all the distinctive elements of the genre,' combined with tense exploration, supernatural threats, ominous cult imagery, and a horror-focused abandoned-island setting. The gameplay loop centers on exploring Godstone, investigating the protagonist's father's disappearance and occult rituals, using a mystic pendulum to interact with and modify the environment, and crucially avoiding direct combat: 'Alan always avoids direct combat' and progresses via 'Hide and Sneak' stealth mechanics. That strongly supports survival_horror as the primary identity, with psychological_horror and stealth_action_adventure as meaningful secondary overlaps.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Samson as a single-player open-world urban action game, not a pure racer. The core loop centers on moving through open world city districts, taking consequential jobs/missions under a debt-and-time-pressure structure, engaging in close-quarters brawler combat, and using cars as traversal/combat tools during chases and collisions. Repeated signals include 'Open World Districts,' 'Brawler Combat,' 'Cars as Weapons,' climbing through alleys and rooftops, a 25+ upgrade skill tree, law-response escalation, and story-driven progress tied to Samson's sister and criminal debt. Racing is present, but the evidence frames driving as one major verb inside a broader open-world action-adventure campaign rather than the sole governing loop.

resolved_similar_games.why_similar: Open-world city crime structure with hand-to-hand combat, driving, police pressure, and story missions centered on street-level violence.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Accepted as western_narrative_rpg using DLC inheritance guidance. Terran Armada is explicitly described as a story DLC for Starfield, adding new locations, technologies, weapons, and enemy encounters, with no indication of a mode or genre shift. The parent game's stored descriptions strongly establish the core identity: Bethesda-style single-player sci-fi RPG with character creation, faction and side quests, dialogue-driven roleplay, open-ended planetary exploration, ship piloting/combat, loot/crafting, and outpost building across a long-form campaign. That evidence supports a quest-driven open-world western narrative RPG more strongly than a pure shooter or action-adventure classification. The DLC copy is additive and fully consistent with that parent gameplay identity.

resolved_similar_games.why_similar: Terran Armada is additive story DLC built directly on Starfield’s open-world sci-fi RPG structure, progression, exploration, and combat systems.
Very close Bethesda-style first-person open-world RPG formula with quest hubs, looting, shooting, crafting, and player-driven character builds.
Single-player first-person sci-fi RPG centered on questing, dialogue choices, companions, exploration, and ranged combat.
Another Bethesda open-world RPG emphasizing freeform exploration, faction questlines, character building, and long-form solo progression.
resolved_similar_games.resolved_title: The Elder Scrolls V: Skyrim VR

resolved_similar_games.why_similar: Spacefaring action RPG with planetary exploration, sci-fi combat, progression, and story-driven missions, though less sandbox-driven than Bethesda games.

title: ChainStaff
anchor_summary: A single-player 2D action-platformer built around a transforming melee/ranged traversal weapon, with grappling movement, brutal combat, boss fights, upgrades, and a heavy-metal presentation.

similar_games.title: Valfaris
similar_games.why_similar: Brutal 2D action-platforming against alien monstrosities with heavy combat, big bosses, weapon upgrades, and a metal-forward vibe.

similar_games.title: Bionic Commando Rearmed
similar_games.why_similar: Shares the grappling-centric traversal/combat identity, where swinging and weapon use are tightly fused into platforming encounters.

similar_games.title: The Dishwasher: Vampire Smile
similar_games.why_similar: A fast, gory 2D action game with aggressive melee combat, stylish violence, and a similarly nasty hand-drawn feel.

similar_games.title: Blasphemous
similar_games.why_similar: Another brutal side-scrolling melee action-platformer focused on punishing combat, grotesque enemies, and memorable boss fights.

similar_games.title: Foregone
similar_games.why_similar: Comparable side-scrolling action-platformer structure with fast combat, upgrade-driven progression, and a campaign built around repeated fights and bosses.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently identifies ChainStaff as a 'brutal action-platformer' and '2D Platformer' with a transforming spear/grappling weapon, swinging and traversal, mutating hordes, and 'bone-shaking boss fights.' The core loop is side-scrolling combat plus platforming through a handcrafted campaign. The text also supports a single-player premium campaign structure, hand-drawn surreal presentation, grotesque sci-fi/horror setting, and some authored branching via rescue-or-harvest choices leading to different endings. Evidence is strong for action_platformer and does not strongly support a different primary archetype such as metroidvania or character_action.

resolved_similar_games.why_similar: Brutal 2D action-platforming against alien monstrosities with heavy combat, big bosses, weapon upgrades, and a metal-forward vibe.
Shares the grappling-centric traversal/combat identity, where swinging and weapon use are tightly fused into platforming encounters.

title: Eko and the Bewitched Lands
anchor_summary: Fast-paced single-player action roguelite/action-RPG built around repeated runs, dodge-heavy combat, randomized upgrades, elemental abilities, relics/artifacts, and some puzzle/exploration structure with meta progression between runs.

similar_games.title: Hades
similar_games.why_similar: Run-based action roguelite with fast dodging, aggressive real-time combat, build-shaping upgrades, and persistent progression between attempts.

similar_games.title: Wizard of Legend
similar_games.why_similar: Element-focused roguelite combat with quick dashes, randomized runs, relic loadouts, and heavy emphasis on ability combinations.

similar_games.title: Ember Knights
similar_games.why_similar: Top-down hack-and-slash roguelite with brisk movement, dodge-centric fights, run upgrades, and artifact-driven build variation.

similar_games.title: Ravenswatch
similar_games.why_similar: Action roguelike centered on repeat runs, hero ability builds, environmental exploration, and meta-style progression in a fantasy setting.

similar_games.title: Children of Morta
similar_games.why_similar: Action RPG/roguelite hybrid with persistent progression, dungeon runs, loot/upgrades, and a stronger adventure structure than pure arcade roguelikes.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Store and aggregator descriptions consistently identify a single-player action RPG with randomized worlds, roguelike run structure, fast dodge-heavy combat, bosses, weapons/abilities/artifacts, per-run upgrades, and permanent meta progression. That strongly supports an action-RPG identity with replayable build variation. The main ambiguity is taxonomy fit: the game reads like a single-player action roguelite, but that exact archetype is not available in the allowed list, so loot_action_rpg is the closest supported primary archetype, with character_action as secondary due to fast aggressive dodge-based combat.

resolved_similar_games.why_similar: Run-based action roguelite with fast dodging, aggressive real-time combat, build-shaping upgrades, and persistent progression between attempts.
